confidence.disclosure: Saladin's life is unusually well documented because he had two contemporary biographers — his judge and confidant Baha al-Din ibn Shaddad, who travelled with him for years, and his secretary Imad al-Din al-Isfahani — both of whom recorded events in real time. The major battles (Hattin, 4 July 1187), the surrender of Jerusalem (2 October 1187), the Treaty of Jaffa (September 1192) and his death (4 March 1193) are all dated to the day. His earlier movements between Tikrit, Mosul, Baalbek, Damascus and Egypt are well attested by year, less so by month. The night of his birth and the family's expulsion from Tikrit is a tradition reported by Ibn Shaddad rather than a documented fact, but it is unanimous in the sources.

sources: Baha al-Din ibn Shaddad, al-Nawadir al-Sultaniyya wa-l-Mahasin al-Yusufiyya
Imad al-Din al-Isfahani, al-Fath al-Qussi fi al-Fath al-Qudsi
Malcolm Cameron Lyons & D.E.P. Jackson, Saladin: The Politics of the Holy War (1982)

journey.event: Born in the citadel of Tikrit on the Tigris, where his Kurdish father Najm al-Din Ayyub serves as warden. According to Ibn Shaddad, Saladin is born on the very night his family is expelled from Tikrit after his uncle Shirkuh kills a friend of the local Seljuk governor.

The family moves to Mosul, where Imad al-Din Zangi — the Turkish ruler of northern Iraq and Syria — acknowledges his debt to Ayyub for once sheltering his defeated army at Tikrit, and takes him into his service.
Ayyub is appointed commander of the Zangid fortress of Baalbek in the Bekaa Valley. Saladin spends part of his childhood here, in the shadow of the Roman temples.

When Nur al-Din, Zangi's son, takes Damascus, Ayyub is rewarded with command of the city. Saladin grows up in Damascus at the heart of Nur al-Din's anti-Crusader court, receiving an education in the religious sciences alongside military training. He later said he was fond of the city all his life.
Joins his uncle Shirkuh on the first of three Zangid expeditions to Egypt, sent by Nur al-Din to support the failing Fatimid caliphate against Crusader pressure.
After Shirkuh's death, Saladin is appointed vizier of the Fatimid caliphate in Cairo at the age of 32 — a surprising compromise choice between Egyptian factions and Nur al-Din's commanders.

On the death of the last Fatimid caliph al-Adid, Saladin formally abolishes the Shi'i Fatimid caliphate and restores the Friday sermon in the name of the Sunni Abbasid caliph in Baghdad. He is now effective ruler of Egypt.

Nur al-Din dies. Saladin breaks with the Zangid succession, declares himself sultan of Egypt and Syria, and begins the long project of uniting the Muslim Near East against the Crusader states. Sends his brother Turan-Shah to conquer Yemen.
Marches from Egypt to Syria and enters Damascus, which accepts him without resistance. He establishes the city as one of his two main capitals alongside Cairo.
Marries Ismat al-Din Khatun, the widow of Nur al-Din and daughter of the former Damascene ruler Unur, securing dynastic legitimacy through ties to two ruling houses.
Captures Aleppo from the remaining Zangids in May 1183 after long negotiation, completing his control of Syria. With Egypt and Syria now united under one banner, the Crusader states are encircled.

Brings Mosul to terms after a long campaign and a serious illness that nearly kills him in early 1185. Signs a peace treaty in March 1186 with the city's Zangid ruler. Now master of a unified Muslim front from the Nile to the Tigris.

On 4 July 1187, at the Horns of Hattin near Tiberias, Saladin destroys the main Crusader army of the Kingdom of Jerusalem. The Frankish king Guy is captured; Reynald of Châtillon, who had broken every truce, Saladin executes with his own hand. The True Cross is taken.

On 2 October 1187 — by a striking coincidence the anniversary of the Prophet's Night Journey to Jerusalem — the city surrenders to Saladin after a short siege. Despite his earlier vow of vengeance for the Crusader massacre of 1099, he allows the Christians to ransom themselves and forbids any reprisal.

In the months after Jerusalem, Saladin sweeps through the Crusader cities of the coast: Acre, Sidon, Beirut, Caesarea, Nazareth, Jaffa, Ascalon — all fall in succession. Only Tyre, behind its sea walls, holds out as a rallying point for the Latin survivors.
The Third Crusade arrives. Guy of Lusignan begins the siege of Acre in August 1189. Saladin's land army besieges the besiegers in turn, and the city becomes the centre of a long, grinding war.
Acre falls to the Crusaders in July 1191 after Richard the Lionheart and Philip of France arrive. Saladin loses the open battle of Arsuf to Richard in September. Withdraws to a war of attrition.

Signs the Treaty of Jaffa with Richard the Lionheart in September 1192. Jerusalem remains in Muslim hands; Christian pilgrims are guaranteed safe access. Richard departs for Europe in October. The war is over.

Dies in Damascus on 4 March 1193, exhausted by years of campaigning and a fever he could not throw off. He had given away most of his personal wealth; his friends found that the most powerful ruler in the Muslim world had not left enough to pay for his grave. Buried in a mausoleum next to the Umayyad Mosque, where he still lies.
